summary: McKenzie reports dramatic early results from Google's new Conversion Optimizer for AdWords, which switches bidding from cost-per-click to cost-per-action on the content network. His daily ad spend dropped 50% while click-through rates improved, cutting his cost per acquisition from 45-50 cents to 25 cents. He walks through the unit economics showing how this doubled his profit margin without any effort. He also flags the strategic risk: by telling Google exactly what a conversion is worth, advertisers hand Google the information needed for perfect price discrimination.
key_points: Google's Conversion Optimizer cut advertising costs by 50% while improving CTR by 50%, dramatically improving unit economics
At 45-50 cents per trial, Google and content publishers captured most of the value from each sale; at 25 cents, profit margins doubled
CPA bidding naturally aligns incentives between Google, content publishers, and advertisers by rewarding relevant ad placements over spam
The strategic risk is that sharing conversion values with Google gives them the data needed for perfect price discrimination, eliminating consumer surplus
The optimizer effectively solves the spam site problem by automatically directing spend away from low-converting placements
money_quotes: This is the best thing to happen to AdWords since AdWords.

If I pay 45 cents for a trial, then I am paying $18 to make a sale worth approximately $24 to me. I still make money, but Google and the content publisher get most of the value from the sale, and I do all of the work.
That doubles my profit margin without me lifting a finger, other than to turn Conversion Optimizer on.
For once the interests of Google, the content provider, and the advertiser are in perfect alignment here.
Visitors at Mrs. Lindle's Reading Tips are much more likely to want to play bingo today than folks reading snazzygurl413's MySpace page.
To do this, Google needs to know exactly how much a conversion is worth to me. That would, theoretically, allow them to achieve the holy grail of monopolies: perfect price discrimination.
When Google does things right, wow, do they do things right.
My advertising has gone from barely above water to, what is the vernacular, making money hats.
key_insight: CPA-based advertising aligns incentives across the entire ad ecosystem by rewarding relevant placements, but the tradeoff is handing your monopolist supplier the exact information they need to extract maximum value from you.
